coredata.dc:title: Rabies virus glycoprotein-amplified hierarchical targeted hybrids capable of magneto-electric penetration delivery to orthotopic brain tumor
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Yu-Lin
Kuo, Li-Wen
Hsu, Chia-Hsien
Chiang, Chi-Shiun
Lu, Yu-Jen
Chang, Shing-Jyh
Hu, Shang-Hsiu
coredata.dc:description: Compact nanohybrids can potentially unite various therapeutic features and reduce side effects for precise cancer therapy. However, the poor accumulation and limited tumor penetration of drugs at the tumor impede the manifestation of nanomedicine. We developed a rabies virus glycoprotein (RVG)-amplified hierarchical targeted hybrid that acts as a stealthy and magnetolytic carrier that transports dual tumor-penetrating agents incorporating two drugs (boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs)/doxorubicin and pH-responsive dendrimers (pH-Den)/palbociclib). The developed RVG-decorated hybrids (RVG-hybrids) enhance the accumulation of drugs at tumor by partially bypassing the BBB via spinal cord transportation and pH-induced aggregation of hierarchical targeting. The penetrated delivery of dual pH-Den and B-GQD drugs to deep tumors is actuated by magnetoelectric effect, which are able to generate electrons to achieve electrostatic repulsion and disassemble the hybrids into components of a few nanometers in size. The synergy of magnetoelectric drug penetration and chemotherapy was achieved by delivery of the B-GQDs and pH-Den to orthotopic tumors, which prolonged the host survival time. This RVG-amplified dual hierarchical delivery integrated with controlled and penetrated release from this hybrid improve the distribution of the therapeutic agents at the brain tumor for synergistic therapy, exhibiting potential for clinic use.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The schematic illustration of hierarchical targeting RVG-hybrids via an aggregation transition in the weak acidity. The magnetoelectric disassembly of RVGhybrids facilitates the penetrative delivery of dendrimers and GQDs. (B) Through intravenous injection, RVG-hybrids delivered with (I) neuronal pathway and (II) hierarchical targeting (aggregation transition) at tumor. (III) In vivo magnetoelectric disassembly of the RVG-hybrids for drug penetration deep into tumors.

Synthesis and characterization of the RVG-hybrids. (A) The schematic illustration of synthetic process of the RVG-hybrids. TEM image of (B) BGQDs and (C) hybrids (pH-Den/B-GQD composites). (D, E) TEM images of pH-responsive dendrimers at pH 7.4 and 6.6. (F) Size distribution of G2 PAMAM dendrimers and pH-Den at pH 7.4 and 6.6 measured by DLS. (G) Zeta potential of B-GQDs and pH-Den at pH 7.4 and 6.6 (n = 4, ⁎p < .05). (H) Photoluminescence of B-GQDs. (I) Size distribution of B-GQD nanoclusters, hybrids and RVG-hybrids.

Morphology transformation of RVG-hybrids. TEM images of RVG-hybrids at (A) pH 7.4 and (B) pH 6.6. TEM images of RVG-hybrids treated by (C) 15 min and (D) 30 min of HFMF. (E) Size distribution of the RVG-hybrids at pH = 7.4 and 6.6 measured by DLS. (F) Zeta potential of hybrids and RVG-hybrids at pH 7.4 and pH 6.6 (n = 4). (G) Zeta potential distribution of hybrids after 30 min HFMF at pH 6.6 (n = 5). (H) Fluorescence intensity of B-GQDs, hybrids and RVG-hybrids without and with 30 min HFMF treatment. (I–K) 3D images of a tissue-like hydrogel treated by RVG-hybrids with HFMF for 0, 15 and 30 min. The red fluorescne indicates B-GQD, and cyan represents pH-Den. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) In vitro cell viability of B-GQDs and pH-Den (n = 6). (B) Flow cytometry analysis of RVG-hybrids after 1 h and 4 h of incubation in ALTS1C1 cells. (C) The cell viability of RVG-hybrids, RVG-hybrids[Dox] and RVG-hybrids pH-Den/B-GQD, Dox-hybrids without and with 15 min of HFMF treatment (charging) at different concentrations (n = 6). CLSM images of cellular uptake of (D, E) B-GQDs and (G, H) RVG-hybrids after 1 h and 4 h of incubation. RITC-labeled B-GQD fluorescence (red) was detected in the cytoplasm (green). The nuclei represented in blue. (F, I) Upon 5 min of charging (HFMF treatment), the spread of particles was observed in cells from the B-GQD and RVG-hybrid groups. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The photograph of chip for formation of multicellular tumor spheroids (MTSs). (B) The CLSM image of an ALTS1C1 tumor spheroid. (C) The transparent CLSM image of a tumor spheroid. (D) The center layer of tumor spheroid clipped by Z-stacks of CLSM. (E, F) CLSM images of hybrids treated with ALTS1C1 MTSs. The spheroids co-cultured with hybrids for 4 h were treated with 10 min of HFMF (charging). The colour red indicated B-GQDs, and cyan indicated pH-Den. The scale bar is 50 μm. 3D reconstructed image of tumor spheroid subjected by hybrids (G) without and (H) with HFMF treatment (charging). (I) Fluorescence images of the MTSs incubated with Pb and Dox-loaded hybrids: without chrging and with 20 min of charging. The colour represents anticancer drugs, where red fluorescence was Dox and green fluorescence was Pb. (J) Flow cytometry of anticancer drug delivery in MTSs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Animal study of RVG-hybrids in a brain tumor-bearing mouse. (A) IVIS images of brains at 24 h postinjection of Cy5.5-labeled B-GQDs, B-GQDs+Den, pH-Den, hybrids and RVG-hybrids through tail vein (i.v.) injections. The scale bar is 3 mm. (B) IVIS images of spinal cords after treatment with Cy5.5-labeled RVG-hybrids, hybrids and PBS at 24 h postinjection. The scale bar is 2 mm. (C) Quantification of B-GQDs, B-GQDs+Den, hybrids and RVG-hybrids in brains (n = 6). CLSM images of spine cord treated with (D) PBS, (E) hybrids and (F) RVG-hybrids at 24 h postinjection. The scale bar is 100 and 20 μm for left and right panels, respectively (n = 5). (G) Liver function of ALT and ALP, as well as (H) kidney function of BUN and CRE at 72 h postinjection (n = 5). (I) Survival curve profiles ALTS1C1 tumor-bearing C57B6/L mice after treatment with PBS, free drugs Pb/Dox, RVG-hybrids[Dox], and RVG-hybrids[Pb/Dox] (n = 6). (J) H&E staining of brain tumors after treatment with free drugs Pb/Dox and RVG-hybrids[Pb/Dox] at 20 days postinjection. The scale bar is 1 mm.

The CLSM images of B-GQDs and pH-Den in orthotopic brain tumors after being treated with (A) PBS, (B) B-GQDs, (C) B-GQDs+Den, (D) hybrids, (E) RVG-hybrids and (F) RVG-hybrids with 30 min of HFMF treatment in brain tumors (n = 6). Tumor blood vessels stained in CD31 appear in green, the nucleus is represented in blue, B-GQDs are shown in red and pH-Den is displayed as cyan. The scale bar is 20 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) IVIS images of mice brain tumor. 2 μL of luciferase (LUC) expressing ALTS1C1 cells (6 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6 J. The animals were injected intravenously with 150 μL of RVG-hybrids solution (1 mg/mL for each nanomaterial) at 12th, 16th and 20th days. After 24 h, the treated groups were applied with 30 min of HFMF treatment (n = 6). (B) The releative photon intensities of brain tumor at various treatments. (C) Survival curve profiles LUC-ALTS1C1 tumor-bearing C57B6/L mice after various treatments (n = 6).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compact nanohybrids can potentially unite various therapeutic features and reduce side effects for precise cancer therapy. However, the poor accumulation and limited tumor penetration of drugs at the tumor impede the manifestation of nanomedicine. We developed a rabies virus glycoprotein (RVG)-amplified hierarchical targeted hybrid that acts as a stealthy and magnetolytic carrier that transports dual tumor-penetrating agents incorporating two drugs (boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs)/doxorubicin and pH-responsive dendrimers (pH-Den)/palbociclib). The developed RVG-decorated hybrids (RVG-hybrids) enhance the accumulation of drugs at tumor by partially bypassing the BBB via spinal cord transportation and pH-induced aggregation of hierarchical targeting. The penetrated delivery of dual pH-Den and B-GQD drugs to deep tumors is actuated by magnetoelectric effect, which are able to generate electrons to achieve electrostatic repulsion and disassemble the hybrids into components of a few nanometers in size. The synergy of magnetoelectric drug penetration and chemotherapy was achieved by delivery of the B-GQDs and pH-Den to orthotopic tumors, which prolonged the host survival time. This RVG-amplified dual hierarchical delivery integrated with controlled and penetrated release from this hybrid improve the distribution of the therapeutic agents at the brain tumor for synergistic therapy, exhibiting potential for clinic use.

Rabies virus glycoprotein-amplified hierarchical targeted nanohybrids (RVG-hybrids) transporting graphene quantum dots and pH-responsive dendrimers perform magnetoelectric penetrating delivery of dual drug on demand in vivo, which overcomes dense tumor tissue and delivers deep into the tumors to kill cancer cells.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Versatile hybrids with high therapeutic utility are currently of significant interest for ameliorating the therapeutic efficacy and alleviating the side effects of treatments for tumor therapy. However, the challenges of tumor therapy for effective targeting and heterogeneous distribution of tumor cells still exist. The therapeutic particles have not only to avoid the recognition of reticuloendothelial system in bloodstream but also to overcome dense extracellular fibers around the tumor and high interstitial fluid pressure (IFP) of tumor (caused by supportive tumor stroma, angiogenesis, and fibroblast and matrix contraction), which leads low drug accumulation and inadequate drug penetration throughout the tumor [1–3]. Instead of the versatile vehicles improving therapeutic efficacy, most negatively charged surfaces and large-sized particles have introduced major obstacles for penetration and delivery of drugs [4–8]. Therefore, developing delivery systems to achieve effective tumor accumulation and homogeneous distribution of therapeutic drugs in tumor tissue is indispensable for enhanced tumor therapy.

A simple strategy to engineer particle penetration into deep tumors is to reduce the particle sizes and modify their physicochemical properties [9,10]. When particle size is smaller than a few nanometers, penetrated delivery can be improved [11,12]. On the other hand, designing a particle with tumor-microenvironmental responses is another potential approach to overcome the delivery obstacles caused by tumor heterogeneity [13,14]. Toward this end, the use of hierarchical targeted zwitterionic particles with tumor-environment-sensitive and adjustable charges was recently proposed [15]. The hierarchical targeted particles can modulate their surface charges, hydrophobic/lipophilic characteristics, or integrated molecular functions to sense tumor microenvironment conditions (pH 6 to 7) to enhance the tumor-retention efficacy [16]. A charging-transition of nanogel, nanohybrid or dendrimer has been exhibited to increase their tumor retention through inducing hierarchically aggregation resulted from decreasing the electrostatic repulsion between particles at tumor microenvironment acidity [17]. Based on this strategy, the hierarchical targeted cationic dendrimer partially modified with 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronic acid (PBA) generates a pH-responsive zwitterionic surface, which is able to lead the charging-transition in weak acidity [18]. Furthermore, dendrimer nanoparticles with tunable charges and sizes under external stimuli have each been explored for penetrated drug delivery to deep tumors [19]. Via the surface functional group modification, the assembly and disassembly can be achieved in a small range of pH variation. Thus, engineering particles with strong physical driving forces that can overcome their geometric structure limitations and pass through the physiological extracellular matrix of tumors is critical for cancer therapy.

Ultrathin 2D inorganic graphene oxide (GO), with unique corners or asperities and abundant irregular edges, have also received great attention because it facilitates cell membrane penetration [20,21]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one form of GO with ultrasmall sizes, atomic thickness, photoluminescence and good stability, are potential candidates for penetrated drug delivery [22,23]. However, photothermal therapy of GO-based materials suffers from the low penetrative depth of light in tissue (e.g., near-infrared with a wavelength of 808 nm), limiting clinical applications. Recently, the magneto-electric effect was applied to induce electric currents (eddy currents) via an alternating magnetic field in graphene-based materials to release drugs from nanocarriers [24] or to promote osteogenesis and neurogenesis for cell proliferation [25], whereas only a few studies have focused on transmembrane capability in vivo. The integration of compact particles with two-dimensional GQDs to deliver high payloads of drug as well as conquer tumor heterogeneity can probably improve the permeability of the therapeutic agents at the targeted tumor.

Despite recent exciting advances, the difficulty of penetrating delivery through the tumor-associated blood-brain barrier (BBB) still exists due to the tight junctions of unique brain endothelial cells and efflux transporters. Drugs or cargoes >500 Da that exhibit poor solubility in lipids are usually blocked by the BBB; thus, 98% of small molecules are hampered from entry into the brain [26]. Accordingly, designing particles with BBB-bypassing functions might provide a feasible approach to overcome the BBB [1]. Recently, it has been shown that mimicry of viral molecules such as rabies virus glycoprotein (RVG) causes virions to be delivered to the central nervous system through specific binding to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors on neuronal cells, achieving a delivery system that bypasses the BBB. BBB bypassing is meant that delivery systems can ignore the wall of blood-brain barrier owing to the RVG glycoprotein delivery from a new way by nicotinic acetylcholine receptor [27,28]. Through this approach, RVG-guided tandem peptide nanoparticles transporting siRNA and other delivery systems were able to downregulate genes in neuronal cells in traumatic brain injuries [29–33]. Therefore, integrating RVG into a BBB bypass system with functional features is currently of considerable interest for targeted brain disease therapy.

Here, we developed RVG-decorated hybrids (RVG-hybrids) composed of pH-responsive dendrimers (pH-Den)/boron-doped graphene quantum dot (B-GQD), which can transport dual drugs to (palbociclib (Pb) embedded in dendrimers and doxorubicin (Dox) anchored on B-GQDs) as well as amplify the accumulation of orthotopic glioblastoma via RVG-guided and hierarchical targeting (Fig. 1A). At tumor microenvironments (pH 6.6), the hierarchical targeting enhances tumor accumulation of hybrids through the charging-transition to induce aggregation. Then, the energy of B-GQDs by HFMF induced electron currents (Lenz's law) disrupts the hybrid structure through electrostatic repulsion, dissembling the hybrids into pH-Den and B-GQDs of a few nanometers in size. Through intravenous injection (Fig. 1B), RVG-hybrids delivered with (I) neuronal pathway, (II) aggregation transition at tumor and (III) HFMF-responsive disassembly improve sufficient drug delivery and exhibit the penetration to deep tumor for brain tumor treatments, respectively. Applying this magnetoelectric effect at brain through HFMF, the hybrids can be controlled remotely and precisely, revealing negligible side effects in vivo. Furthermore, the dual drugs, Pb and Dox, showed synergistic effect in cancer cell lines, improving the therapeutic effects. Such virus-inspiration amplified hierarchical delivery integrated with controlled-release penetration from versatile particles can accomplish a well-distributed tumor treatment and suppress tumor growth via synergistic chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: 4-Vinylphenylboronic acid (Sigma-Alrich, CAS Number: C2156-4-9), Boric acid (Sigma-Alrich, CAS Number: 10043-35-3), G2 polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer, ethylenediamine core, generation 2.0 solution (Sigma-Alrich, CAS Number: 93376-66-0), 4-(Bromomethyl) phenylboronic acid (Sigma-Alrich, CAS Number: 68162-47-0), Dimethyl sulfoxide (Merck, 67-68-5), Hydrogen peroxide solution (Sigma-Alrich, CAS Number: 7722-84-1), RVG-9R trifluoroacetate salt (YTIWMPENPRPGTPCDIFTNSRGKRASNGGGGRRRRRRRRR trifluoroacetate salt) (Sigma-Aldrich, CAS Number:213546-53-3), Dox (ChemScene, CAS Number: 23214-92-8), Pb (Sigma-Aldrich, CAS Number: 571190-30-2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron-modified graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (75 mg) and boric acid (150 mg) were dissolved in a mixed solution of 2.5 mL ethanol and 15 mL acetone. After 30 min ultrasound sonication (K031 DELTA DC200 Ultrasonic Cleaner) at room temperature, 2.5 mL of H2O2 (30 wt%) was gradually placed to the solution. Them, the sonicator with a power of 130 W was applied to the mixture for 10 min and added to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Next, the mixture was heated to 200 °C for 24 h in the oven. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. The resultant product was dialyzed with de-ionized water for 3 days (OrDial D-Clean Dialysis Membranes MWCO:1000 Da, Orange) at room temperature. After the dialysis, B-GQDs which were adsorbed on the dialysis membrane was then scraped to a 1.5 mL eppendorf and well sonicated in 1.0 mL ethanol for 30 min (K031 DELTA DC200 Ultrasonic Cleaner). Well-dispersed B-GQDs solution was then dried to obtain B-GQDs powder. The characteristics of B-GQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM-2010, with 104 μA of applied current and 200 kV of acceleration voltage), nanosizer instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS), fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700), superconducting quantum interference device (MPMS-XL). 200 kV of field emission cryo-TEM (JEOL JEM-2100) with applied current 104 μA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pH-sensitive dendrimers (pH-Den)
ce:section.ce:para: The modification of Den followed the reference [18]. 4-(Bromomethyl) phenylboronic acid was reacted with G2 PAMAM dendrimers in dimethyl sulfoxide at 80 °C for 24 h. The weights of 4-(bromomethyl) phenylboronic acid and G2 PAMAM dendrimers are 4.4 mg and 100 mg, respectively. The yielding products were dialyzed with de-ionized water for 3 days (OrDial D-Clean Dialysis Membranes MWCO:1000 Da, Orange) at room temperature to remove remained 4-(bromomethyl) phenylboronic acid, followed by lyophilization to obtain pH-sensitive dendrimers powder. The morphology, particle sizes and functionalization ratio, and the change of the size and zeta-potential at the different PB buffers (pH = 7.4, 6.6) of pH-sensitive dendrimers were analyzed by Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2010), Nanosizer instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS), and 1H-Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (BRUKER AVANCE 500 NMR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characteristics of rabies virus-inspired pH-Den/B-GQDs hybrids (RVG-hybrids)
ce:section.ce:para: B-GQDs (in 1 wt% ethanol: de-ionized water) and pH-Den (in de-ionized water) were well dissolved as the concentration 1 mg/mL, respectively. Dox was loaded on B-GQD by π-π stacking and Pb was loaded in G2 PAMAM dendrimers core by a hydrophobic core. Briefly, 1 mL of Dox (0.1 wt%) solution and 1 mL of B-GQDs (0.1 wt%) were mixed together at room temperature in de-ionic water. Then, the mixture was stirred for 12 h, and the free Dox was removed by dialysis for 24 h. Dox was calibrated by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700; excitation at 480 nm and emission at 578 nm). Furthermore, 1 mL of Pb (0.1 wt%) solution and 1 mL of pH-Den (0.1 wt%) were mixed together in de-ionic water. Pb was calibrated by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700; excitation at 420 nm and emission at 490 nm). The encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity were shown in the supplementary materials. pH-Den which comprises the ability to aggregate in the tumor microenvironment and B-GQDs which comprises the ability to induce electron current to stimulate drug release was mixed by 10 s of sonication (130 W), and the hybrids were formed as B-GQDs were encapsulated in the pH-Den shell.

For RVG functionalization, 2.06 nM RVG glycoprotein (29 amino acid fragment derived from rabies virus glycoprotein (RVG), YTIWMPENPRPGTPCDIFTNSRGKRASNGGGGRRRRRRRRR trifluoroacetate salt; molecular weight is 4843 g/mol) was added into the 1 mg/mL of hybrids solution with a 30 s vortex. After the overnight reaction, dual drug (Dox and Pb) loaded and RVG-hybrids were obtained. The final product was characterized for the changes of the size and zeta-potential at the different PB buffers (pH = 7.4, 6.6) by Nanosizer instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS), and the changes of the form before and after high frequency magnetic field by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700) and Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, JEM-2010).

The disassembly of B-GQD/pH-Den was evaluated by the variation of fluorescence from B-GQDs. The particles were dispersed in PBS solution and estimated the fluorescence intensity by fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700) with an excitation wavelength at 380 nm and emission wavelength at 490 nm. The concentration of B-GQDs in solutions was fixed at 1 mg/mL. For in vitro Dox and Pb release experiment, Dox-loading or Pb-loading prticles were placed in dialysis tubing and immersed in 3 mL of different of pH value phosphate buffer solution (PBS, containing 0.1% w/v, pH 7.4 and 6.8). At different time points, Dox was calibrated by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700; excitation at 480 nm and emission at 578 nm) and Pb was evaluated by Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-2700; excitation at 420 nm and emission at 490 nm). For the HFMF treatment groups, the solution containing drug-loading particles in dialysis tubing was placed in HFMF coils (at a frequency of 50 kHz and a power of 70 W with a strength of 4 kA/m) at the beginning of 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake, distribution, and cytotoxicity of hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Two cell lines, ALTS1C1 and bEnd.3 cell lines, were used in in vitro study. ALTS1C1 cells was a mouse astrocytoma cell line (BCRC number: 60582), and bEnd.3 cells was a mouse cerebral micro-vascular endothelial cells (ATCC® CRL-2299™). ALTS1C1 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium, containing 10% of fetal bovine serum, 1.5 g/L sodium bicarbonate. ALTS1C1 cells were maintained in 37 °C incubator (Water-Jacketed 3010 CO2) under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 supply. bEnd.3 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium, containing 10% of fetal bovine serum. The other culture condition was similar to ALTS1C1. All experiments were performed in the logarithmic phase of cell growth.

For cell uptake study, ALTS1C1 cells were cultured onto a square glass coverslips (Ø18 mm) in 6-well tissue culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well to investigate the cellular distribution of hybrids. For tracking, RITC was applied to label B-GQDs, and then, the RITC-labeled B-GQDs were incorporated to hybrids. The resulting particles at a concentration of 20 μg/mL were incubated with ALTS1C1 cells for 24 h. Then, the cells were fixed and stained with 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and Alexa Fluor 488 Phalloidin (F-actin). After the staining, the excess chemicals were washed by PBS for several times and mounted with the Fluoromount™ aqueous mounting medium solution (Sigma-Aldrich). For quantification of the cellular uptake of particles, ALTS1C1 cells were seeded into 6-well tissue culture plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well. The cells were incubated with RITC-labeled hybrids at a concentration of 20 μg/mL for 1 h and 4 h. Next, flow cytometry (BD FACSCANTO) was used to measure the fluorescence of cells and the data were evaluated by a WinMDI 28 software.

The cell toxicity of pH-Den and B-GQDs was evaluated by PrestoBlue® cell viability assay. ALTS1C1 cells were cultured into 96-well tissue culture plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 100 μL of cell culture medium. The concentration of hybrids for cytotoxicity were ranged from 2.5 to 20 μg/mL. After 24 h of incubation, the cell viability was evaluated by 10 μL PrestoBlue® per well. With 2 h of incubation of PrestoBlue®, the live cells converted the blue oxidized form of the dye (resazurin) into a red-fluorescent reduced form. Then, the cells were evaluated by the Infinite M1000 PRO multimode microplate readers with an excitation wavelength at 570 nm and emission wavelength at 600 nm. For testing the cell viability of hybrids under HFMF, the particles were incubated with ALTS1C1 cells for 4 h in advance, and then, 15 min of HFMF was applied to the cells. ALTS1C1 cells were cultured into 96-well tissue culture plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in 100 μL of cell culture medium. After 24 h of incubation (HFMF was treated after 4 h at the time of the saturated uptake), the cell viability was evaluated by 10 μL PrestoBlue® per well.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Orthotopic intracranial tumor study
ce:section.ce:para: Animal use and experimental protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of National Tsing Hua University (Approval No. 10704). To evaluate the accumulation and penetration efficiency of hybrids, 2 μL ALTS1C1 cells (5 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6 J (female). After two weeks, the animals were injected intravenously with 150 μL RVG-hybrids solution (1 mg/mL for each nanomaterial). After 24 h, the treated groups were applied with 30 min high frequency magnetic field. After 72 h, all animals were anesthetized, heart blood collected, and then sacrificed. Brains were embedded in O.C.T. and sectioned with the thickness~10 μm at −20 °C, and put onto slides (Leica CM1510–3). The tissue slides were then fixed in the −20 °C methanol for 5 min and washed by PBS. To avoid non-specific binding of antibodies, the tissues were immersed in the blocking buffer containing 4% FBS, 1% GS in PBS for 1 h at room temperature. Next, the samples were stained by CD31 (Purified Rat Anti-Mouse Clone MEC 13.3 (RUO) BD Pharmingen 1:150 dilution) for imaging blood vessels at 4 °C for 24 h. Afterward, the samples were washed with PBS for several times and incubated with secondary antibodies (Rabbit anti-Rat IgG (H + L) Cross-Adsorbed Secondary Antibody, Alexa Fluor 488 Invitrogen 1:200 dilution) which was conjugated with CD31 for 1 h at room temperature. The samples were examined by a Carl Zeiss laser scanning confocal microscope (ZEISS LSM 800; Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) which was equipped with a Plan-Neofluar (20×/0.5) and a Fluar objective (40×/1.30 and 63/1.40, oil immersion, Zeiss). The laser sources included 405, 488, 561 and 670 nm for DAPI (emission at 470 nm), GFP (emission at 510 nm), RITC (emission at 580 nm) and Cy5.5 (emission at 470 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution and survival rate with synergistic chemotherapy in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the survival rate with synergistic chemotherapy, 2 μL ALTS1C1 cells (5 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6 J. After 14 days of tumor growth, the animals randomly grouped into four groups (n = 6) were injected intravenously with 150 μL of PBS, dual drug Dox and Pb, RVG-hybrids[Dox], and dual drug (Dox and Pb) loaded RVG-hybrids (1.0 mg/mL for RVG-hybrid[Dox/Pb], which contains 29.2 μg Dox and 58.7 μg Pb/1 mg RVG-hybrids. For comparison, the other drug-treated groups were applied similar drug concentrations.). After 24 h, all groups were applied with 30 min high frequency magnetic field at a frequency of 50 kHz and a power of 70 W with a strength of 4 kA/m. The body weights were examined using an electronic balance in the interval of two days. The survival rates of all groups were recorded every day from the day of ALTS1C1 cells implantation.

The biodistribution of particles were evaluated by the similar animal model. At 14 days postimplantation of tumor, the animals randomly grouped into two groups (n = 6) were injected intravenously with 150 μL PBS, RVG-hybrids, and hybrids solutions (pH-Den was loaded with Cyanine 5.5 and B-GQD was loaded with Rhodamine B isothiocyanate as the fluorescent tracker; 1.0 mg/mL for each nanomaterial). After 24 h, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and brain) were collected and imaged by IVIS spectral imaging system (200 Series, Caliper Life Science, U.S.A.). An IVIS Spectrum imaging system obtained fluorescence images via various conditions (Cy5.5 channel, excited wavelength = 675 nm, emission wavelength = 720 nm; RITC channel excited wavelength = 570 nm, emission wavelength = 620 nm). Furthermore, analysis of blood biochemistry was evaluated by 20 μL of whole blood through AmiShield (ProtectLife International Biomedical Inc., Taiwan), where the blood was withdrawn via submandibular blood collection.

For multiple injections of hybrids to orthotopic tumor-bearing mice, 2 μL of luciferase (LUC) expressing ALTS1C1 cells (6 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6J. The animals were injected intravenously with 150 μL RVG-hybrids solution (1 mg/mL for each nanomaterial) at 12th, 16th and 20th days. The figures were plotted by OriginPro 8.0 and Prism 6.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of B-GQDs and pH-Den
ce:section.ce:para: This hierarchical nanocomposite mainly consisted of two elements, boron-doped graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) and pH-responsive dendrimer shells (pH-Den), as shown in Fig. 2A [34]. The binding energy at 190.8 and 191.7 eV as investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy suggested the formation of carbon‑boron bonds, indicating the doping of boron in the B-GQDs (Fig. S1). The morphology of the B-GQDs was evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Because of hydrophobic interactions, the 2–3 nm B-GQDs tended to aggregate as nanoclusters under TEM (Fig. 2B and Fig. S2). Furthermore, B-GQDs serve as potent drug absorbents that can be loaded with anticancer drugs, i.e., Dox, via π-π interactions. The π-π stacking interactions were applied to modify carbon allotropes such as graphene oxide sheet and carbon nanotubes [35]. Through the mechanism, the loading capacity (LC) of Dox can reach approximately 15.5 mg in 100 mg of B-GQD and the loading efficiency (LE) is 91% (Table S1).

A pH-responsive dendrimer (pH-Den) was fabricated by conjugating 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronic acid (PBA) to G2 PAMAM dendrimers at molar ratios of 9:1 in organic solvent at 80 °C (Fig. 2A) [18,36]. After 24 h, H1 nuclear magnetic resonance analysis revealed that nine amine groups on the G2 PAMAM dendrimers were replaced by PBA (termed the 9-PBA dendrimer, see Fig. S3). To evaluate the size variability in different environments, 9-PBA dendrimers were suspended at pH 7.4 and 6.6 (similar to the pH in the tumor microenvironment). GQDs and pH-Den were integrated together by electrostatic attraction for hierarchical targeting and remotely control penetrated delivery. After this self-assembly process, the morphology of the resulting hybrids examined by TEM exhibited unique core-shell structures (Fig. 2C). The darker clusters as the core in the image represented the aggregation of B-GQDs, which was surrounded by pH-Den shells that were 150 nm in diameter. Upon closer observation, the irregular shape of the B-GQD cluster is probably attributed to π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions between two-dimensional nanosheets. Although the cluster's shape is irregular, the size distribution of the resulting particle is still maintained at 50 to 100 nm in diameter owing to the surface energy balance. Once the clusters were formed, the absorption of pH-Den was carried out, forming the uniform shells on the surface of the clusters through secondary interactions.

The TEM images revealed that the self-assembled 9-PBA dendrimers were narrow distributed, with a diameter of approximately 80 nm (Fig. 2D). In contrast, once the particles were placed in a weakly acidic environment, they tended to aggregate to a larger size than that in neutral solution, and the size could reach approximately 500 nm (Fig. 2E). The aggregation also reflected the size distribution of the 9-PBA dendrimer under various conditions (Fig. 2F). As expected, an obvious size conversion occurred by slightly tuning the pH to make the solution weakly acidic, and the sizes increased ten-fold and displayed a sharp distribution. The zeta potential of the pH-responsive dendrimer clusters was negative at pH 7.4 and showed obvious changes from −9.1 eV to +0.5 eV at weak acidity (Fig. 2G). On the other hand, the excitation-dependent photoluminescence spectra of the B-GQDs was also obtained, where the emission wavelength of 468, 475, 481, 490, 503, 518, and 522 nm was observed by the excitation wavelength of 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440 and 460 nm, respectively (Fig. 2H) [37].

These dendritic boxes are able to incorporate hydrophobic therapeutic agents by utilizing host-guest interactions inside their empty cavities present around the core [38]. In pH-Den, the hydrophobic G1 inhibitor Pb could be embedded in the core of the dendrimers via hydrophobic interactions, and the LC of Pb was calculated to be approximately 8.1 mg in 100 mg of pH-Den and the LE is 96%, where Pb is the first CDK4/6 inhibitor to be approved as a cancer therapy by FDA and acts as a selective inhibitor of the cyclin-dependent kinases CDK4 and CDK6 [39]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of Pb was provided in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect of Pb and Dox to ALTS1C1 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic effects of Pb with chemotherapeutic agents have been documented to enhance the tumor suppression in clinics [40,41]. For the mechanism of Pb combination therapy, Shane Whittaker et al. reported their combination therapy with Pb and radiation for glioblastoma [42], and the possible mechanism for the combination of Pb and Dox can be drawn from the inhibition of cell cycles [43]. To evaluate the synergistic effect of Pb and Dox to ALTS1C1 cells (a mouse astrocytoma cell line) in our study, the combinatorial effects of these two agents were carried out and analyzed by Chou-Talalay analysis with variety of combination ratios [44]. The results indicated that Pb can synergize with dox with a various ratio for ALTS1C1 cells (see Table S2). Compared to other groups, the molar ratio of Pb:Dox is 1:2.5 exhibited the most comprehensive and potent synergy [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size distribution of RVG-hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: DLS analyzed the size distribution of the B-GQDs and the hybrids (Fig. 2I). With the incorporation of the B-GQDs in the core of pH-Den, the average size of the resulting hybrids similar to pH-Den particle alone is about 105 nm. Furthermore, 2.06 nM RVG glycoprotein (29 amino acid fragment derived from rabies virus glycoprotein (RVG), YTIWMPENPRPGTPCDIFTNSRGKRASNGGGGRRRRRRRRR trifluoroacetate salt; molecular weight is 4843 g/mol) was absorbed on hybrids through the positive domain of RRRRRRRRR. Once the RVG conjugating on hybrids, the size distribution of RVG-hybrids with a mean size of 115 nm was slightly larger than that of hybrids (Fig. 2I). The fluorescence spectra revealed that the binding efficiency is more than 95% (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition of hybrids in acidic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and surface properties of hybrids while surface charge-switching were evaluated under various conditions. The TEM images in Fig. 3A and B show the morphologies of RVG-hybrids at pH 7.4 and 6.6, respectively. As expected, the behavior of pH-responsive aggregation was identical to that of pH-Den particles alone and did not affect the incorporation of B-GQDs in the core and RVG conjugation owing to the dendrimeric encapsulation and the relatively low portion of RVG coverage (2.06 nM of RVG on 1 mg hybrids). Further, in higher magnification TEM images, the B-GQD cluster at pH 7.4 became well dispersed in the pH-Den at pH 6.6. The appearance presumably was induced by the protonation of B-GQDs under the weakly acidic conditions, leading to disassembly of the B-GQDs in pH-Den (insets of Fig. 3A and B, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration depth of hybrids by HFMF
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the magnetoelectric effects on the hybrid size transition, the hybrids were subjected to HFMF treatment for 15 and 30 min at a power of 70 W (50 kHz). In clinic, similar instrument integrated with magnetic nanoparticles developed by MagForce are current approved for use in Europe to treat glioblastoma multiforme [46,47]. Through heat dissipation of iron oxide particles actuated by the magnetic field, the treatment of local hyperthermia at brain tumor prolonged survival time from 15 to 23 months from clinical trials [48,49]. As shown in Fig. 3C and D, after 15 min of charging (HFMF treatment), most GQDs were released from the RVG-hybrids, suggesting severe conformational changes in the composites. After increasing the HFMF treatment duration to 30 min, most of the hybrids were deformed and destroyed, and the components of the B-GQDs and pH-Den were randomly spread on the carbon film. The tissue penetration of magnetic signal is a critical issue in this study. Here, HFMF is a coil with a diameter of 2 cm at a power of 70 W (50 kHz). Once the sample and animal were placed in the coil, the strength is able to actuate the magnetoelectic disassembly of RVG-hybrids. However, outside of the coil exhibited the low intensity of HFMF, which is not able to induce the disassembly of particles. To treat brain tumor, only the head of mice was placed in the coil so the spinal cord would not be affected.

The size distribution of RVG-hybrids changed from approximately 120 to 530 nm at pH 7.4 to pH 6.6, which revealed the size transition under slight environmental changes (Fig. 3E). The size transition was also observed by hybrids without RVG conjugation (Fig. S6). Similar to pH-Den, reduced electrostatic repulsion in the RVG-hybrids induced the size transition attributed to the surface of the pH-Den from −5.7 eV to 2.0 eV at weak acidity (Fig. 3F). Furthermore, parts of RVG potentially detached from the hybrids in acidic condition. After RVG functionalization, the negative charge on the surface of hybrids was decreased by nine arginine residues of RVG. Furthermore, both particles maintained the good stability in in fresh homologous serum for one week (Fig. S7).

Based on Lenz's law, the direction of current in a conductor can be induced by changing the magnetic field because the external magnetic field generates a magnetic field. The changes in magnetic flux can induce the electromotive force in the conductors. By Lenz's law, eddy currents are induced in closed loops in conductors within nearby stationary conductors by an electromagnetic effect [50–52]. To evaluate these magnetoelectric effects on the B-GQDs, the zeta-potentials of particles were measured after HFMF treatment for 15 min. As shown in Fig. 3G, zeta-potentials obviously changed to negative due to the increased electrons on the particles. Even though the vehicles were treated with HFMF for 40 min, only a slight temperature increase was observed (Fig. S8). Furthermore, once the bulky graphene-based sheet was placed close to the HFMF, the eddy currents could be immediately generated (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triggered release of drugs from hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The accumulative release study of Dox from RVG-hybrids were investigated at pH 7.4 and 6.8. As shown in Fig. S10, the release patterns were almost identical under pH 7.4 and 6.8. Despite the conformation of hybrids was changed at the weak acidic environments (Fig. 3B), the dox release was not affected by the changes, suggesting that the strong affinity between Dox and B-GQDs at pH 6.8 was still maintained. Once subjecting the particles to HFMF for 15 min, the obvious release of Dox was observed. The release was also attributed by the currents on the particles which generated the opposite charges to release the Dox. At the weak acid environments (pH 6.8), the slower release of Pb was obtained than that in pH 7.4. The reason was probably attributed by the aggregation of hybrids under pH 6.8. Under 15 min of HFMF treatment, the release was slightly faster than that without treatment. The photoluminescence of the B-GQDs was evaluated to further confirm B-GQD disassembly caused by the magnetoelectric effect. The hybrids and RVG-hybrids exhibited a relatively weaker fluorescence intensity than the B-GQDs because of the carbon-shielding effects of the B-GQDs after self-assembly, as shown in Fig. 3H. After subjecting the particles to 30 min of HFMF, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, indicating disassembly of hybrids and reduction of shielding effects. Accordingly, the changes of morphology, surface charge and fluorescence changes indicated the induced currents on B-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration of hybrids in a tissue prosthesis
ce:section.ce:para: A tissue prosthesis was utilized to evaluate the penetration effect of hybrids after HFMF treatment. Three-dimensional fluorescence images of a collagenous tissue (10 wt% collagen) exhibited the particle penetration behaviors for various treatments. Two-dimensional nanosheets have been shown to penetrate cell membranes through the sharp edges and corners of the nanosheets [20]. Before HFMF treatment, the hybrids were spread on the surface of a prosthesis, and only a few fluorescence signals were detected, as shown in the images in Fig. 3I. After 15 min of charging (HFMF treatment), the fluorescence signals from both the B-GQDs and the pH-Den infiltrated from the gel surface to deeper in the gel and were distributed from strong to weak (Fig. 3J). The widespread fluorescence signals after the charging time was increased to 30 min are shown in Fig. 3K. Because the depth of penetration in a gel is the hundred micrometers, 15 and 30 min of HFMF treatment were applied to investigate the behaviors. This wide penetration results from a combination of the hybrid disassembly and ultrasmall sizes induced by currents on the particles, enhancing the permeability and penetration and improving the distribution of drugs in the tissue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell uptake of hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of both pH-Den and B-GQDs was examined by incubating particles with ALTS1C1 cells (a mouse astrocytoma cell line) for different lengths of time. At 100 μg/mL of both particles, the cell viability was maintained at almost 97% (Fig. 4A). Subsequently, the intracellular uptake of the hybrids was examined by incubating them with ALTS1C1 cells. The results of flow cytometry revealed the numbers of hybrids in the cytoplasm of ALTS1C1 cells after 1 h of incubation, and increased fluorescence signals of the hybrids could be detected when the incubation time was increased to 4 h (Fig. 4B). For the in vitro study, in the drug loading groups, Pb and Dox were loaded onto pH-Den and B-GQDs before the formation of hybrids. Various amounts of resulting hybrids were cultured with cancer cells for 24 h and treated with HFMF for 15 mins or were untreated. Fig. 4C shows the cell viability versus concentration, the loading capacity is 2.92 mg of Dox in 100 mg of hybrids, and 2.29 mg Dox/5.87 mg Pb in RVG-hybrids (molar ratio of Pb/Dox in RVG-hybrids is 2.7:1; the combination index (CI) of Pb/Dox = 2.7:1 is 0.71.). The reason of 15 min of MF treatments was due to the disassembly and zeta potential change of hybrids. Furthermore, the drug release was observed at pH 6.8 while applying 15 min of MF. The results exhibited that Pb/Dox improved the cell-killing effects compared to those of a single drug (only Dox), especially at low concentrations. Once the particles were uptaken by endocytosis, the drug would release from the particles owing to the degradation of particles in the endosome and lysosome. Therefore, even without HFMF, it would be able to induce cytotoxicity. Moreover, with HFMF stimulus, the disassembly promoted ultrasmall component delivery and enhanced the efficacy of treatment at hybrid concentrations of 5.0 and 10 μg/μL. Other cytotoxicity studies of ALTS1C1 cell line including the hybrids loaded with Pb or Dox under HFMF treatment were also provided in Fig. S11. Several conclusions can be obtained from the study. First, the carriers along exhibited the low toxicity after the incubation. Second, the carriers loaded with Pb displayed the weak cell killing effect, which might be caused by the embedded Pb in carriers. Third, once combining Dox to carriers, the cell viability decreased with an increase of concentration. With the assistance of HFMF, the cell killing effects were improved.

B-GQD clusters and hybrids were incubated with ALTS1C1 cells for various time periods to verify the cell uptake. Red fluorescence dye (RITC) was used to label the B-GQDs to track their intracellular behaviors. CLSM images of ALTS1C1 cells revealed that only a few B-GQDs could be observed in the cytoplasm and that the amount of hybrids was higher than the amount of B-GQDs in the cell after 1 h of incubation (Fig. 4D and G), indicating that the aggregation of B-GQDs decreased the rate of cellular uptake. With the incubation time increased to 4 h, both the B-GQDs and the hybrids showed higher accumulation in cells compared with that after 1 h of incubation (Fig. 4E and H). After treating the cells with HFMF for 5 min of charging, the separated B-GQDs were dispersed in the cytoplasm for both the B-GQDs alone and the hybrids due to the potential penetrating effect in the cells (Fig. 4F and I). The reason of 5 min of HFMF treatment was due to the short distance of disassembly of particles in a single cell.

bEnd.3 cells (mouse cerebral micro-vascular endothelial cells) usually used for most of BBB evaluation were applied to estimate the uptake and cytotoxicity of hybrids and RVG-hybrids [53]. As shown in Fig. S12A, the uptake of hybrids and RVG-hybrids was observed in the cytoplasm after 1 h of incubation. With the incubation time increased to 4 h, both particles exhibited higher accumulation in cells. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of particles to bEnd.3 cells was also evaluated. The hybrids at various amounts were incubated with bEnd.3 cells for 24 h, and then, treated with HFMF for 15 min or were untreated. In Fig. S12B, the results displayed that Pb/Dox increased the cell toxicity compared to those of a carrier along or single drug. However, when compared to ALTS1C1 cell line, the lower toxicity was observed for drug loaded groups, which potentially reflected to low cell uptake of particles in bEnd.3 cells when compared to ALTS1C1 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetration of hybrids in tumor spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: Ex vivo multicellular tumor spheroids (MTSs) of approximately 120 μm in diameter were constructed on a chip to estimate the tumor penetration of the pH-Den and B-GQDs (Fig. 5A) [54]. The chip contained 480 wells, each 300 μm in diameter, for cell spheroid formation. By injecting 5 × 106 cells/mL into the chip, ALTS1C1 cells were trapped in the 300-μm wells within 20 min (Fig. S13A). After 24 h of incubation, uniform tumor spheroids were formed and used for further study (Fig. S13B and S13C). Similar to an in vivo tumor, the MTSs possessed a heterogeneous cell density, and the compact cancer cells around the spheroids latently decreased the permeation of drugs or cargos to the inner regions of the tumors (Fig. 5B, C and Supplementary Video 1). Fig. 5D displayed that the center layer of tumor spheroid possessed the dense shell constructed by cytoskeleton.

Ex vivo multicellular tumor spheroids (MTSs) of approximately 120 μm in diameter were constructed on a chip to estimate the tumor penetration of the pH-Den and B-GQDs (Fig. 5A) [54]. The chip contained 480 wells, each 300 μm in diameter, for cell spheroid formation. By injecting 5 × 106 cells/mL into the chip, ALTS1C1 cells were trapped in the 300-μm wells within 20 min (Fig. S13A). After 24 h of incubation, uniform tumor spheroids were formed and used for further study (Fig. S13B and S13C). Similar to an in vivo tumor, the MTSs possessed a heterogeneous cell density, and the compact cancer cells around the spheroids latently decreased the permeation of drugs or cargos to the inner regions of the tumors (Fig. 5B, C and Supplementary Video 1). Fig. 5D displayed that the center layer of tumor spheroid possessed the dense shell constructed by cytoskeleton.

In Fig. 5E, hybrids composed of pH-Den and B-GQDs were incubated with the MTSs for 4 h, and most signals from the pH-Den and B-GQDs were observed around the MTSs. After 10 min of HFMF treatment, 2 nm of dendrimers (cyan) and 2–5 nm of B-GQDs (red) with bright fluorescence could be detected inside the MTSs (Fig. 5F). Additionally, 3D and 2.5D reconstructed images of CLSM images representing the fluorescence intensity distribution demonstrated homogeneous penetration of both dendrimers and B-GQDs after charging (HFMF treatment) (Fig. 5G and H; Fig. S14A). After the tumor spheroids were treated by HFMF for 15 min, an increase of fluorescence intensity of RITC-labeled B-GQDs in supernatant was observed, suggesting the liberation of B-GQD actuated by HFMF (Fig. S14B). The behaviors and distribution revealed that the morphology and nanosized effects of both the pH-Den and B-GQDs enhanced their tumor-penetrating abilities and improved their delivery of drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug transportation of hybrids in BBB-mimetic multicellular tumor spheroids
ce:section.ce:para: The similar chip was used for ex vivo co-cultured of bEnd.3 cells (mouse cerebral micro-vascular endothelial cells) and ALTS1C1 cells of multicellular tumor spheroids (MTSs) for estimating the BBB penetration [55] As shown in Fig. S15A, after the formation, hybrids and RVG-hybrids were injected to the MTSs chips to evaluate the interactions. In Fig. S15B, both hybrids and RVG-hybrids (labeled by RITC) were incubated with co-cultured MTSs for 4 h, and most fluorescence signals from particles were observed around the MTSs. Compared to hybrids, the relative fluorescence intensity of RVG-hybrids was approximately twice greater (Fig. S15C). Furthermore, after 15 min of HFMF treatment, CLSM images and the relative fluorescence intensity in MTSs reveals that the distribution of parts of particles penetrated into MTSs (Fig. S15B and S15C). However, under similar treatment, the penetration depth of hybrids in co-cultured MTSs was slightly lower than that in MTSs.

To track the synergistic therapy effect after HFMF treatment, the hybrids loaded with Pb/Dox with green and red fluorescence were gently injected into the chip. Without HFMF treatment, the rigid structure of the cell spheroids was not damaged, even though the hybrids with fluorescence indicating Pb and Dox were distributed in the MTS, as shown in the left panel of Fig. 5I, where the excitation wavelength of Pb was 405 nm and the emission wavelength was ranged from 500 to 550 nm. About 65% of cells extracted from the MTS chip were killed after treated by RVG-hybrids with HFMF, measured by ELISA investigation (Fig. S16).

15 min of HFMF was applied to the tumor spheroids after 4 h at the time of the cellular uptake on a chip. Then, after another 20 h of incubation, the cell viability was evaluated by PrestoBlue®. Fig. S17 exhibited the cell killing results, where Hybrid[Dox/Pb] represents the highest drug payload in hybrids (2.92 mg Dox and 5.87 mg Pb in 100 mg hybrids); Hybrid[0.1Dox/Pb] means that 10% of highest drug payload in hybrids (0.292 mg Dox and 0.587 mg Pb in 100 mg hybrids). When compared hybrid[0.1Dox/Pb] to hybrid[Dox/Pb], the cell viability decreased from 89% to 67%, indicating that the higher drug concentrations could improve the cell death in tumor spheroids. Once subjecting the tumor spheroids to HFMF, an increase of cell killing effects was observed for both hybrid[0.1Dox/Pb] and hybrid[Dox/Pb] group. The results were actuated by the drug release and penetration under HFMF as demonstrated in the previous observation. Flow cytometry results displaying an increased fluorescence uptake in ALTS1C1 cells in the MTSs further confirmed the disassembly fostering the penetrated delivery (Fig. 5J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo targeted accumulation of hybrids in brain tumor and spinal cord
ce:section.ce:para: The charge-switching and RVG-guiding effects of hybrids was estimated in a orthotopic brain tumor model in vivo. Briefly, 100 μL of hybrids or RVG-hybrids at 1 mg/mL, both labeled with Cy5.5, was injected intravenously into orthotopic brain tumor-bearing mice. After 24 h of injection, the fluorescence images captured by in vivo imaging system (IVIS) were applied to estimate the biodistribution of hybrids. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of RVG-hybrids in the brain tumor was stronger than that in other groups (Fig. 6A), implying the improvement of RVG-guided targeting probably by enabling crossing of the BBB. Furthermore, compared to B-GQDs, B-GQD+Den and pH-Den, the enhanced accumulation was observed in hybrids group, indicating the accumulation improvement of hierarchical targeting of hybrids.

The obvious signal of hybrids at brain tumor was induced by two potential mechanisms. First, the leakages between vascular endothelial cells due to the angiogenesis at tumor enhanced the accumulation and retention time of particles (known as EPR effect). Second, the hierarchical targeted effect of hybrids was actuated at the tumor site through the size transition of the hybrids in the weakly acidic environment, where the increased size of the hybrids further improved their residing time at the targeted site [18,19]. Expecting an excitement in developing in hierarchical targeted delivery, the dual RVG-guided targeting bypassed the BBB to enhance the accumulation efficiency of the hybrids in the tumors. Fig. 6B displayed the IVIS images of spinal cords, the delivery pathway of the nervous central system, demonstrating exhibited ten-fold improvement of RVG-hybrids in transporting in the spinal cords compared with that of hybrids without RVG. Considering the disruption and bypass of the BBB via RVG, the accumulation of the RVG-hybrids at brain tumor was higher than that of the hybrids and other groups (Fig. 6C). The results demonstrated that the combination of RVG-guided targeting and pH-responsive aggregation could be seen as an approximately four-fold improvement for the B-GQDs or pH-Den alone. The organ accumulation of hybrids and RVG-hybrids was also provided in Fig. S18.

The cy5.5-labeled hybrids were also applied to evaluate the real time accumulation at brain tumor through IVIS. As shown in Fig. S19, the accumulation of cy5.5-labeled hybrids were increased with time. To evaluate the RVG effects in targeting, free RVG was served as inhibitors to investigate the real time accumulation of RVG-hybrids in the brain tumor [56]. RVG-hybrids exhibited a significantly deceased accumulation in brain after free RVG blocked the receptors in Fig. S20. At 24 h post-injection, both signals of RVG and hybrids were observed at the tumor, suggesting the good affinity between two components in Fig. S21. The weaker fluorescence signal in RITC channel was caused by the low penetration of excited light (RITC channel excited wavelength = 570 nm, emission wavelength = 620 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking and analysis of hybrids in spinal cords
ce:section.ce:para: The spinal cords were collected and examined by CLSM at 24 h postinjection to understand the RVG-mediated delivery, where glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), an intermediate filament protein of 52 kD of glial cells, was stained in green and nuclei stained by DAPI in blue. In Fig. 6D, no significant signal from particle was detected in spine cord after PBS treatment. Once the hybrids and RVG-hybrids were intravenously subjected to the animal via tail vein, the obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the spine cord in Fig. 6E and F (Supplementary Video 2). The results exhibited that RVG-guided targeting (RVG-hybrids) could be an approximately four-fold improvement for hybrids. An increase of particle fluorescence was observed in spine cord once the particles conjugated to RVG glycoprotein, indicating the effective transport in the central nervous system through the specific binding of nicotinic receptors on neuronal cells [56,57]. Other studies also demonstrated that rabies virus uptake was mediated by clathrin-mediated endocytosis [58]. The RVG glycoprotein potentially bypasses neuron junctions to peripheral nerves, and is transported in axonal to the central nervous system [59]. The large area of images of spine cord was also provided to confirm the large amount of RVG-hybrids at spine cord (see Fig. S22A and S22B). Some of the fluorescence of particles were observed around the cell nuclei. To investigate the rate of cell death, TUNEL staining was performed on RVG-treated animals. Compared to TUNEL positive cells in control section, RVG-hybrids treated group demonstrated approximately identical TUNEL signal, suggesting the low cytotoxicity in spinal cord (Fig. S22C). Furthermore, it also worth to mention that there are no obvious inflammatory responses in the brains and spine cords after the particle treatment (see Fig. S23).

The spinal cords were collected and examined by CLSM at 24 h postinjection to understand the RVG-mediated delivery, where glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP), an intermediate filament protein of 52 kD of glial cells, was stained in green and nuclei stained by DAPI in blue. In Fig. 6D, no significant signal from particle was detected in spine cord after PBS treatment. Once the hybrids and RVG-hybrids were intravenously subjected to the animal via tail vein, the obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the spine cord in Fig. 6E and F (Supplementary Video 2). The results exhibited that RVG-guided targeting (RVG-hybrids) could be an approximately four-fold improvement for hybrids. An increase of particle fluorescence was observed in spine cord once the particles conjugated to RVG glycoprotein, indicating the effective transport in the central nervous system through the specific binding of nicotinic receptors on neuronal cells [56,57]. Other studies also demonstrated that rabies virus uptake was mediated by clathrin-mediated endocytosis [58]. The RVG glycoprotein potentially bypasses neuron junctions to peripheral nerves, and is transported in axonal to the central nervous system [59]. The large area of images of spine cord was also provided to confirm the large amount of RVG-hybrids at spine cord (see Fig. S22A and S22B). Some of the fluorescence of particles were observed around the cell nuclei. To investigate the rate of cell death, TUNEL staining was performed on RVG-treated animals. Compared to TUNEL positive cells in control section, RVG-hybrids treated group demonstrated approximately identical TUNEL signal, suggesting the low cytotoxicity in spinal cord (Fig. S22C). Furthermore, it also worth to mention that there are no obvious inflammatory responses in the brains and spine cords after the particle treatment (see Fig. S23).

At 7 days postinjection, RVG-hybrids could still maintain the strong accumulation at brain tumor when compared to hybrid group but almost be cleared in spine cord in Fig. S24A and S24B, suggesting that the particles were transported in spine cord rather than long-time accumulation. Furthermore, to evaluate the excretion of RVG-hybrids in brain, the accumulation in brain was also monitored at 14 days postinjection of RVG-hybrids. As shown in Fig. S24C, the signal of RVG-hybrids was obviously decreased, indicating the excretion of RVG-hybrids. The possible mechanism of excretion was caused by the immune response in brain tumor, which was confirmed by the observation of IBA1 stained microglia/macrophage cells (Fig. S24D). On the other hand, pharmacokinetics data for the blood half-life was evaluated by ICP mass. Blood retention of RVG-hybrids for 24 h were estimated by the quantitative determination of Cd by ICP-MS, and the blood was withdrawn via submandibular blood collection. The tracing particles express PK as fraction of signal recovered in the first blood draw taken as 100% in an ALTS1C1 tumor-bearing C57B6/L mouse model. In Fig. S25, RVG-hybrids displayed 35% and 6% retention after 2 and 24 h postinjection, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic efficacy of hybrids in orthotopic tumor-bearing mice
ce:section.ce:para: Survival rates of mice with various treatments including PBS, free dual drugs (Pb/Dox), Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment, and Pb/Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment were carried out to evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of RVG-hybrids. Before treatment, 2 μL ALTS1C1 cells (5 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6 J. At two weeks postinjection of cancer cells, 150 μL of solution containing drug or particles were injected intravenously to mice via tail vein. The amount of free drug was identical to that embedded in the RVG-hybrids for each groups. The liver functions including alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) were investigated at 72 postinjection of drug. The differences between PBS groups, free drug treatments and RVG-hybrid groups was relatively small, suggesting low toxicity of various treatments (Fig. 6G). Furthermore, the kidney functions such as blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine (CRE) demonstrated similar profiles (Fig. 6H).

The group treated with Pb/Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF displayed the longest prolonged survival time, followed by the Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment, free dual drugs and PBS (Fig. 6I). The prolonged survival time in the group treated with Pb/Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment was attributed to the combination of RVG-guided targeting to enhance the dose of drugs delivered to the brain tumor and HFMF-triggered penetrated delivery for the complete, homogeneous distribution of drugs. Furthermore, the weight of each mouse measured in the interval of 7 days did not exhibit obvious changes, indicating reasonable biocompatibility (see Fig. S26). H&E staining of the main organs in each group were also evaluated as shown in Fig. S27. Although the slight kidney injury was revealed by H&E staining, the damages were tolerable according to the CRE results. On the other, the HFMF was only applied in brain, which might also mitigate the drug damages on organs. No significant injury was found in other organs, suggesting minimal influence of the treatments.

At 20 days posttreatment, the brain tumor size decreased and the tumor infiltration was decreased by the Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment, and the treatment was more efficient with the combination of drugs, Pb/Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF treatment than in the PBS-treated group (Fig. 6J, and Fig. S28), indicating an obvious ability to treat tumors by penetrated delivery. In summary, the combination of the RVG-guided BBB crossing, pH-transition induced accumulation, and HFMF-triggered permeation of hybrids to brain tumors by RVG-hybrids improve the delivery of drug/therapeutic agents at tumor, overcoming the hindrances from the BBB, ECM, and IPF and realizing a homogeneous, combinational and deeply penetrating chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Penetrated delivery of hybrids under HFMF in orthotopic brain tumor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the penetrated delivery of particles in vivo, various particles including B-GQDs, B-GQD + Den, hybrids and RVG-hybrids were injected intravenously to ALTS1C1 orthotopic brain tumor-bearing C57B6/L mice. For tracking purpose, B-GQDs tagged with Rhodamin displayed in red, and dendrimer labeled by Cy5.5 represents in cyan, respectively. At 24 h postinjection, the brains were collected and stained by DAPI to label nuclei as well as by CD31 to obtain blood vessels (Fig. 7 ), and the brain tumors were evaluated by CLSM. The particle penetration at orthotopic tumor is a proof-of-concept in animal study. Despite the BBB is intact at the early stage of brain tumor, the abnormal vessels led by the metabolic stresses from tumor which direct the vascular endothelial growth and angiogenesis are rapid formation during tumor progression. Therefore, the leakages between interendothelial cells at tumor could be induced and cause the EPR effect of particles in a disrupted blood-brain-tumor-barrier (BBTB) [60]. There is no obvious red and cyan fluorescence signals from the tumor while treating by PBS as shown in Fig. 7A. Furthermore, the brain tumor displayed the high density of endothelial cells labeled by CD31 as representing in green. Consistent to the results of tumor accumulation in Fig. 6A, the hierarchical targeted effect of hybrids improved the tumor accumulation when compared the particles without the ability of size transition (Fig. 7B to D).

With the assistance of RVG, the RVG-hybrids exhibited an increase of fluorescence signals for both B-GQD and pH-Den as shown in Fig. 7E. Before the HFMF stimulus, parts of the RVG hybrids were observed in the periphery of the tumor and blood vessels rather than in the core of the tumor, which was probably caused by the dense extracellular matrix of brain tumors, obstructing the penetrated delivery of the hybrids. Similar results were also observed in the in vitro study of MTSs (Fig. 5E to G). The fluorescence images of B-GQD and pH-Den also indicated that most of particles and blood vessels were overlaid in the brain tumor.

After subjecting the brain to HFMF, both the B-GQDs and the pH-Den were detected in wide regions of the brain tumor (Fig. 7F), and a large portion of the B-GQDs and pH-Den were separated and spread within the tumor. The results indicated excellent tumor penetration of both nanomaterials in vivo after the treatment. After quantifying the fluorescence signals of the brain tumor, the average fluorescence intensities of the B-GQDs and pH-Den were much stronger than those of the HFMF-untreated tumor (Fig. 7E and F).

Under HFMF treating, CLSM image of normal brain area treated by RVG-hybrids at 24 h postinjection revealed that nearly no particle was obtained, which was probably induced the dense tissue structure of normal brain (Fig. S29). To verify the distribution of B-GQD in brain after treated by HFMF, the area including both normal brain tissue and brain tumors were evaluated by CLSM. As shown in Fig. S30A to S30C, compared to normal brain tissue, most of particles were observed in the area of brain tumor. The quantification of fluorescence signals also revealed the similar results (Fig. S30D). The observation of B-GQD at brain tumor after HFMF treatment can be understood by the initial accumulation of particles in brain tumor due to the angiogenesis at tumor enhanced the accumulation and retention time of particles (known as EPR effect) and the hierarchical targeted effect [18,19]. Thus, before HFMF treatment, most of particles were accumulated in brain tumor. The whole brain tumor slices were also investigated in Fig. S31 to confirm the distribution of RVG-hybrids before HFMF treatment, which also demonstrated the major observation of particles were located at tumor. The results demonstrated that the penetrated delivery of B-GQDs and pH-Den in RVG-hybrids can be achieved in vivo by HFMF, delivering the therapeutic agents into deep tumors in mice.

The traditional hyperthermia would cause the tissue damage through the overheating. Most of hyperthermia was generated by the magnetic particles, e.g., iron oxide nanoparticles, under alternating magnetic field treatment. However, in our study, the hybrids performed weak magnetic property so it only a slight temperature increase was observed (approximately increased 5 °C with 40 min of HFMF treatment in Fig. S8). In in vivo study, 30 min of HFMF treatment would increase weak hyperthermia effect in brain (Fig. S32).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiple injections of hybrids to orthotopic tumor-bearing mice
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the therapeutic effects on brain tumor, the dosages were increased by three times treatments for each group. For the tracking purpose, 2 μL of luciferase (LUC) expressing ALTS1C1 cells (6 × 107/mL) were implanted and inoculated intracranially into 6–8 week-old C57BL/6 J. The animals were injected intravenously with 150 μL RVG-hybrids solution (1 mg/mL for each nanomaterial) at 12th, 16th and 20th days. After 24 h, the treated groups were applied with 30 min high frequency magnetic field. The results revealed that the combination treatments of hybrids (two drugs and RVG enhancement) could effectively inhibit tumor growth and prolong the survival time as shown in Fig. 8A. The bioluminescent signal of brain tumor treated by dual drug (Pb/Dox) was 6.4 fold greater than that treated by RVG-hybrid[Pb/Dox] after 24 days (Fig. 8B). Furthermore, the group treated with Pb/Dox-loaded RVG-hybrids with HFMF displayed the longest prolonged survival time (median survival was 37 days), and the median survival of group treated by PBS was 17 days (Fig. 8C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: A hierarchical targeted hybrid has been developed with RVG-amplified and tumor microenvironment-responsive (pH-responsive charge transition) tumor accumulation. The hybrid is assembled by atomic-thin boron-doped graphene quantum dots and few-nanometered dendrimers with good biocompatibility. Decorated by RVG, the hybrids demonstrate not only a spontaneous accumulation at weakly acidic tumor microenvironment but also a BBB-crossing ability via a partially spinal cord transportation. At tumor site, while locally charging by HFMF, the hybrids dissembled into large amounts of individual B-GQDs and dendrimers facilities the penetration to deep tumor. Such magnetoelectric penetration was demonstrated in both in vitro multicellular tumor models and in vivo orthotopic glioblastoma. The successful in vivo enhanced-targeting efficacy and HFMF-triggered penetration of the hybrids amplify the distribution of the cargoes and drugs at the brain tumor for synergistic therapy. The co-administration of dual drug carriers with penetrated chemotherapy accomplished remarkable in vivo tumor inhibition and extended mouse survival rates. Such functional hybrid system may offer a new strategy for cancer therapy, potentially leading to clinic uses.
